demonstrations.title: INNER JOIN DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Inner join returns only rows where there is a match in BOTH tables. Users with orders: INCLUDED, Users without orders: EXCLUDED, Orders with invalid user_id: EXCLUDED
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date
    FROM users u
    INNER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    ORDER BY u.name, o.order_date
demonstrations.result_title: Users who have placed orders (INNER JOIN)

demonstrations.title: LEFT JOIN DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Left join returns ALL rows from left table (users), and matched rows from the right table (orders). All users: INCLUDED, Users without orders: order columns will be NULL, Orders with invalid user_id: NOT shown
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    ORDER BY u.name, o.order_date
demonstrations.result_title: All users, with their orders if they have any (LEFT JOIN)

demonstrations.title: LEFT JOIN EXCLUDING NULLS DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Left join with WHERE clause excluding NULL values returns only rows where there's a match in both tables. Users with orders: INCLUDED, Users without orders: EXCLUDED
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    WHERE o.id IS NOT NULL
    ORDER BY u.name, o.order_date
demonstrations.result_title: Users with orders (LEFT JOIN WHERE order_id IS NOT NULL)

demonstrations.title: RIGHT JOIN DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Right join returns ALL rows from the right table (orders), and matched rows from the left table (users). All orders: INCLUDED, Orders with invalid user_id: user columns will be NULL, Users without orders: NOT shown. Simulated by swapping tables and using LEFT JOIN
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date
    FROM orders o
    LEFT JOIN users u ON u.id = o.user_id
    ORDER BY o.order_date
demonstrations.result_title: All orders, with user info if available (RIGHT JOIN - simulated)

demonstrations.title: FULL OUTER JOIN DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Full outer join returns rows when there is a match in ONE OR BOTH tables. All users: INCLUDED, All orders: INCLUDED, Where there's no match: columns will be NULL. Simulated using UNION of LEFT JOIN results
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    UNION ALL
    SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date
    FROM orders o
    LEFT JOIN users u ON u.id = o.user_id
    WHERE u.id IS NULL
    ORDER BY order_date NULLS LAST, name
demonstrations.result_title: All users and all orders, regardless of matches (FULL OUTER JOIN - simulated)

demonstrations.title: SELF JOIN DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Self join joins a table to itself, using table aliases to distinguish between two instances. All employees: INCLUDED, Shows each employee with their manager, Top-level employees have NULL for manager name
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        e1.id as employee_id,
        e1.name as employee_name,
        e2.id as manager_id,
        e2.name as manager_name
    FROM employees e1
    LEFT JOIN employees e2 ON e1.manager_id = e2.id
    ORDER BY e1.id

demonstrations.title: CROSS JOIN DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Cross join returns the Cartesian product of two tables. Every row from first table × every row from second table. Result has M × N rows. No join condition. Can produce very large result sets!
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.name as user_name,
        p.name as product_name,
        p.price
    FROM users u
    CROSS JOIN products p
    ORDER BY u.name, p.name
demonstrations.result_title: All combinations of users × products (CROSS JOIN)

demonstrations.title: ANTI-JOIN: Users without orders
demonstrations.description: Anti-join finds users that don't have matching records in orders table. Uses LEFT JOIN with WHERE clause checking for NULL. Very useful for finding inactive users
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id,
        u.name,
        u.email
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    WHERE o.id IS NULL
    ORDER BY u.name
demonstrations.result_title: Users who have NOT placed any orders (ANTI-JOIN)

demonstrations.title: ANTI-JOIN: Orphan orders
demonstrations.description: Anti-join finds orders that don't have matching records in users table. Uses LEFT JOIN with WHERE clause checking for NULL. Very useful for identifying orphan records and data integrity issues
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        o.id,
        o.amount,
        o.order_date,
        o.user_id
    FROM orders o
    LEFT JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
    WHERE u.id IS NULL
    ORDER BY o.id

demonstrations.title: JOIN WITH AGGREGATION: Order summary
demonstrations.description: Join with aggregation combines join operations with GROUP BY and aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN). Shows order statistics per user for reporting and analytics
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name,
        u.email,
        COUNT(o.id) as total_orders,
        SUM(o.amount) as total_spent,
        AVG(o.amount) as avg_order_amount,
        MAX(o.amount) as largest_order,
        MIN(o.amount) as smallest_order
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    GROUP BY u.id, u.name, u.email
    ORDER BY u.name
demonstrations.result_title: Order summary per user (JOIN with COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN)

demonstrations.title: JOIN WITH AGGREGATION: Product statistics
demonstrations.description: Join with aggregation combines join operations with GROUP BY and COUNT. Shows how many times each product was ordered. Useful for inventory management and sales analysis
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        p.id as product_id,
        p.name as product_name,
        p.price,
        COUNT(o.id) as times_ordered
    FROM products p
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON p.id = o.product_id
    GROUP BY p.id, p.name, p.price
    ORDER BY times_ordered DESC
demonstrations.result_title: Products ordered with frequency (JOIN with COUNT)

demonstrations.title: MULTIPLE JOINS DEMONSTRATION
demonstrations.description: Multiple joins allow you to connect three or more tables in a single query. Each JOIN links another table. Data flows from one table to the next. Common for complex relationships (users → orders → products)
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.id as user_id,
        u.name as user_name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount as order_amount,
        o.order_date,
        p.id as product_id,
        p.name as product_name,
        p.price as product_price
    FROM users u
    LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    LEFT JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id
    ORDER BY u.name, o.order_date
demonstrations.result_title: Users, their orders, and product details (MULTIPLE JOINS)

demonstrations.title: NON-EQUI JOIN: Greater than comparison
demonstrations.description: Non-equi join uses comparison operators other than equality for joining. This example uses > to find orders where order amount exceeds product price. Common use case: Finding profitable orders
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount as order_amount,
        p.name as product_name,
        p.price as product_price
    FROM users u
    JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id
    WHERE o.amount > p.price
    ORDER BY u.name, o.amount DESC
demonstrations.result_title: Orders where order amount exceeds product price (non-equi with >)

demonstrations.title: NON-EQUI JOIN: Range comparison with BETWEEN
demonstrations.description: Non-equi join uses comparison operators other than equality for joining. This example uses BETWEEN to find orders with amounts in a specific range (100-250). Common use case: Finding orders in a certain price range
demonstrations.query:
SELECT
        u.name as user_name,
        u.email,
        o.id as order_id,
        o.amount as order_amount
    FROM users u
    JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
    WHERE o.amount BETWEEN 100 AND 250
    ORDER BY o.amount
demonstrations.result_title: Orders with amounts between 100 and 250 (non-equi with BETWEEN)
